This normalization means PER is comparable across seasons in terms of “relative to league,” but you still have era artifacts because the mapping from box-score events to “value” might not be stable across eras. So the flow is: Box score → weighted sum per minute (uPER) → pace adjust → normalize to league average (aPER) The formula looks monstrous because it encodes all those weights, plus some league constants to keep the scale stable. If you want a simplified “mental model”: PER likes: high usage with decent efficiency, lots of free throws, rebounds (especially defensive), some assists, some stocks (steals+blocks), low turnovers. PER dislikes: low-usage players, specialists who don’t fill the box score, and players who do one thing elite but don’t rack up multiple categories.
How does Win Shares differ conceptually? People often cite PER and WS like they’re similar “all-in-one” stats but they feel very different.

They’re both “one-number” stats, but they’re built on different philosophies. PER is an individual box-score rate metric. It says: per minute, how much box-score stuff did you produce, with some weights. Win Shares (WS) is a team wins allocation metric derived from Dean Oliver’s framework. It starts from team performance (points scored and allowed, adjusted for pace and efficiency), converts that into estimated team wins, then allocates those wins to players based on their offensive and defensive contributions as inferred from box score. Key differences: 1) Anchor: individual production vs team wins - PER: anchored to individual box-score events, then normalized. - WS: anchored to team outcome (wins) via offensive/defensive efficiency, then distributed. 2) Rate vs cumulative - PER is per-minute; if you want “total value” you multiply by minutes (or use “VORP” in that ecosystem). - WS is cumulative by default; WS/48 is the rate version. 3) Offense/defense split WS splits into Offensive Win Shares (OWS) and Defensive Win Shares (DWS). PER doesn’t split; it’s one blended number with weak defense representation. 4) Defensive estimation WS defense is still box-score-derived and team-context-heavy. DWS uses team defensive rating and minutes plus some box-score components; it’s not measuring “your” defense cleanly. It can credit players on elite defensive teams heavily even if their individual impact is ambiguous. 5) Interpretability WS has a nice narrative hook: “estimated wins contributed.” But it’s not literally causal; it’s an allocation scheme. PER is more “productivity index.” As a result, you’ll see players with modest PER but solid WS if they play huge minutes on great teams with efficient roles, and vice versa. WS is also sensitive to team context; PER is sensitive to individual box-score stuffing.
Can you get into the guts of WS a bit more? Like how do they convert points to wins and then to player shares?

WS is built from a few steps: 1) Estimate team wins from team efficiency Dean Oliver’s “Pythagorean expectation” (or a variant) estimates winning percentage from points scored and allowed, or from offensive/defensive rating. Basketball-Reference uses its own implementation. The idea is: if a team scores X points per 100 possessions and allows Y, that point differential implies an expected win total. 2) Split team wins into offensive and defensive wins They compute “marginal offense” and “marginal defense” relative to a baseline (often league average). Those are turned into “marginal points,” then into “marginal wins” using a points-per-win conversion (which depends on scoring environment). 3) Allocate offensive wins to players via offensive box-score components OWS uses: - Individual scoring efficiency via “marginal points produced” (built from points, shooting efficiency, offensive rebounds, assists, turnovers, etc.) - Team offensive possessions and minutes to apportion credit. It’s tied to the “points produced” concept: a player produces points by scoring and by assisting on teammates’ scores (with some assumptions), and you compare that to a baseline efficiency. 4) Allocate defensive wins to players via minutes and team defense (plus minor adjustments) DWS is more “team defense times minutes share,” with a role for defensive rebounds, steals, blocks, and fouls in the defensive rating estimate, but it’s heavily driven by team defensive rating and playing time. 5) Sum OWS + DWS = WS So WS is very “accounting”-like: start with team results, allocate based on box score. That’s why it’s sometimes criticized: it can’t separate individuals well on defense, and it can over-credit players who happen to play a lot on great teams.
So is WS basically just “minutes on a good team” for defense? That’s the meme I see.

The meme has a kernel of truth, especially for DWS. DWS is strongly correlated with: - Team defensive quality (team defensive rating) - Minutes played Because the defensive side is hard to apportion from box score, the system uses team defense as the “pie” and gives players slices mostly proportional to minutes, with some adjustments. So two players with similar minutes on an elite defense can end up with similar DWS even if one is a defensive anchor and the other is more replaceable. That doesn’t mean DWS is useless—it often “gets the sign right” (players who never play on good defenses won’t rack up DWS), but it’s not a precise individual defender metric. OWS is more individually driven, because offense is better captured in box score (still imperfect, but better). So WS as a whole is a mix: OWS has more signal about individual offense; DWS has lots of team context.
Where does BPM fit in? People say BPM is “box plus-minus” and is closer to impact, but it’s still box score. How does it bridge the gap?

BPM (Box Plus/Minus) is explicitly trying to estimate on-court impact (plus-minus per 100 possessions) using only box-score inputs plus position estimates. It’s a regression model trained to predict regularized adjusted plus-minus (RAPM) style outcomes from box stats. So conceptually: - PER: heuristic weights to summarize productivity - WS: accounting allocation of wins from team efficiency - BPM: statistical model mapping box score → estimated impact on point differential BPM has a few distinctive features: 1) It’s possession-based and impact-oriented BPM is in “points per 100 possessions above average.” That’s directly interpretable as an impact estimate, not just productivity. 2) It uses position and role adjustments BPM estimates position (1 through 5) based on assist rate, rebound rate, etc., then adjusts coefficients so that, for example, assists from a center might be weighted differently than assists from a point guard, because they correlate differently with impact. 3) It includes both offensive and defensive BPM OBPM and DBPM are derived from the same box inputs but separated using a mapping informed by historical relationships. DBPM is still limited—since the box doesn’t see most defense—but it’s trying to approximate impact rather than allocate team defense. 4) It can be “gamed” by box-score patterns Because it’s trained on historical data, players with unusual stat profiles can be misestimated. And because it depends on box stats, it still misses spacing, screening, off-ball defense, etc. BPM is often seen as a better “one-number” than PER because it’s tuned to impact and is on a point differential scale. But modern public impact metrics like EPM (Estimated Plus-Minus), LEBRON, RAPTOR (legacy), DARKO, etc., incorporate tracking data and/or priors and are generally better.
You mentioned normalization for PER. Doesn’t BPM also need normalization? How do they set the baseline?

BPM is centered so that league average is approximately 0.0. In practice, Basketball-Reference calibrates it so that the minutes-weighted average BPM across the league is near zero each season, with some constraints to make team totals align with point differential. There’s a “team adjustment” aspect: if you sum BPM across players on a team weighted by minutes, you want it to roughly match the team’s net rating (with some constant). Since BPM is derived from regression on historical data, it needs calibration to ensure the league and team totals make sense. So yes, both have baselines: - PER baseline: league average fixed at 15 - BPM baseline: league average fixed at 0, on a per-100 possessions scale
Let’s talk flaws more concretely. Give me a player archetype PER overrates and one it underrates, without naming specific players if you don’t want.

Overrated by PER archetype: - High-usage interior scorer/rebounder with mediocre efficiency and limited passing/defense They rack up points, rebounds, maybe some blocks, and because PER rewards those counting stats heavily, they can look great even if their shot selection and turnovers drag team offense and their defense is neutral or negative. PER’s treatment of defensive value is too coarse to penalize them appropriately. Underrated by PER archetype: - Low-usage, high-efficiency spacer/connector who makes quick decisions, keeps turnovers low, and plays strong positional defense without steals/blocks This player might have modest points, rebounds, assists, and few “stocks,” yet their presence improves lineup efficiency via spacing, ball movement, and reliable defense. PER won’t see most of that. Another underrated type: - Elite on-ball creator whose value is creating advantages that lead to hockey assists and rotations, but whose raw assist totals don’t fully capture it (and who might have high turnovers because of role). PER penalizes turnovers heavily without fully crediting the difficulty/creation value.
How does PER treat efficiency? Like if someone scores 25 on meh true shooting vs 20 on elite true shooting, what happens?

PER incorporates efficiency through: - Penalizing missed field goals and missed free throws - Rewarding made shots and made free throws But it’s not explicitly built around true shooting percentage (TS%) or points per shot. It’s a linear combination of makes and misses, which correlates with efficiency but doesn’t perfectly capture shot value differences. In your example: - 25 points on mediocre TS usually implies more shot attempts and/or fewer free throws per shot and/or more misses. PER will reward the extra made shots and points but also penalize the misses. - 20 points on elite TS implies fewer misses and possibly more free throws and/or threes. PER will like the efficiency (fewer negative events) but might still prefer the higher-volume scorer if the volume comes with enough other stats. PER tends to like volume scorers more than impact metrics do, especially if the volume comes with rebounds and some defensive stats, because it’s not explicitly translating scoring into “marginal points above average efficiency.” It’s more “total box output per minute.” Win Shares and BPM generally incorporate efficiency in a way that better reflects marginal value relative to average.
Where does usage rate fit into these? PER seems to correlate with usage. Is that inherent?

It’s partly inherent and partly empirical. PER rewards: - Field goal makes - Free throws made - Assists - Offensive rebounds Those are all things that tend to rise with usage and offensive responsibility. A high-usage player has more chances to generate positive events. Even if efficiency is average, the sheer volume can push PER up, especially if minutes are steady. PER does penalize missed shots and turnovers, which also increase with usage, but historically the weights don’t fully counterbalance the extra positives for many high-usage archetypes. So PER and usage are positively correlated. BPM tries to account for role by including usage-related variables and by being trained against impact; it still correlates with usage (stars have the ball), but it’s less “usage loves me” than PER. Win Shares can correlate with usage too, but it also heavily rewards efficiency and team success, so some high-usage, low-efficiency profiles don’t do as well in WS.
Okay, step aside from these older stats. If I want a “stat-head but not proprietary” view, what’s your hierarchy of public metrics?

If we restrict to widely available/public and reasonably documented: 1) Plus-minus based (best for “impact”) - RAPM (regularized adjusted plus-minus) when you have multi-year samples. It’s noisy year-to-year but conceptually clean: it uses score differentials with player on/off, adjusted for teammates/opponents. - Public derivatives like multi-year RAPM tables. 2) Hybrid impact metrics (public-ish but sometimes partially opaque) - EPM, LEBRON, DARKO: these typically blend box score, plus-minus, tracking, and priors. They’re often the best single-season impact estimates, but the full model details may not be fully open. 3) Box-score impact models - BPM: good baseline, transparent-ish, easy to compute, and on an impact scale. - Some open-source “box RAPM” or “PIPM-like” projects exist, but quality varies. 4) Accounting/wins allocation - Win Shares: decent for summarizing “contributed to team success” in a historical context, but not great for isolating defense. 5) Productivity indices - PER: useful as a quick snapshot of box-score stuffing per minute, but you should mentally adjust for biases. If you’re doing analysis, a good workflow is: start with an impact metric (or a couple), sanity-check with box components (shooting efficiency, turnover rate, rebounding, foul rate), and then check lineup data if you can.
Can we dig into why RAPM is so noisy? People treat it like gospel but then say “small sample” constantly.

RAPM estimates each player’s effect on point differential by solving a regression problem: Net rating (or possession-level scoring margin) = sum of player effects (home team players) − sum of player effects (away team players) + noise The “adjusted” part is controlling for who you play with and against. The “regularized” part (ridge regression, typically) stabilizes estimates when data is insufficient. It’s noisy because: 1) Collinearity / shared minutes Players share the court with certain teammates a lot. If two players are almost always together, the model has trouble separating their individual effects; the estimates can swing. 2) Limited possessions Even a full season is not that many possessions for a single player relative to the variance in scoring margin due to shooting variance, opponent shooting, end-of-quarter heaves, etc. 3) Context changes Lineups change, roles change, injuries/trades (team composition changes), and the model assumes a player’s effect is roughly constant over the sample. 4) Score effects and strategy Teams change behavior depending on score, opponent, and game situation. RAPM usually doesn’t fully model that. Multi-year RAPM reduces noise by increasing sample size and smoothing over context changes, but then you trade off recency. That’s why modern metrics add priors (e.g., aging curves) and box/tracking signals to stabilize single-season estimates.
Since we’re talking analytics: can you explain why TS% became the default efficiency stat and how it should be used?

TS% (True Shooting Percentage) estimates scoring efficiency by incorporating twos, threes, and free throws into one percentage: TS% = Points / (2 * (FGA + 0.44 * FTA)) The 0.44 approximates how many possessions free throws consume (since not all free throw trips are two shots, and some are and-1s, technicals, etc.). Why TS% became standard: - It values threes correctly relative to twos (because points are in numerator). - It includes free throws, which are a major part of efficient scoring. - It’s easy to compute and correlates well with points per possession used. How to use it: - Pair it with volume (usage or scoring rate). High TS on tiny volume can be less impactful than slightly lower TS on huge volume, depending on creation value and team context. - Consider shot profile. Two players with the same TS can get there differently (more threes vs more free throws), which can matter for playoff translation and lineup fit. - Consider “relative TS” (rTS): player TS minus league average TS. That accounts for era.
How would you compare TS% to eFG% for different roles?

eFG% (effective field goal %) is: eFG% = (FGM + 0.5*3PM) / FGA It adjusts for threes but ignores free throws. So: - For players who generate lots of free throws, TS% is more complete. A player can have mediocre eFG% but strong TS% if they live at the line. - For low-FTA players (spot-up shooters, some bigs who finish without drawing fouls), eFG% can be a clean read of shooting efficiency without the noise of free throw rate. - eFG% is also sometimes useful for team-level shot quality discussions because it isolates field goal outcomes. In role terms: - Primary creators: TS% is usually the right headline because free throws are part of their scoring package. - Catch-and-shoot specialists: eFG% and 3P% might be more intuitive, but TS% still works. - Big finishers: TS% captures dunk/layup efficiency plus foul-drawing; eFG% captures finishing on attempts.
Let’s circle back. If PER is basically “box score per minute,” why not just use a linear weights model based on points per possession? Isn’t PER kind of outdated?

Many analysts do prefer linear weights models grounded in possession value. PER is “outdated” in the sense that: - It predates widespread play-by-play and tracking access - It uses heuristic weights and team adjustments that aren’t fully aligned with modern expected points frameworks A modern linear weights model would: - Assign each event a value in points (or points added) based on how it changes expected points on a possession - Convert to per-possession or per-minute impact - Potentially incorporate context (shot location, defender proximity, etc.) However, purely box-score-based linear weights still has the same fundamental limitation: box score doesn’t capture most defense and a lot of offense (screening, spacing, pre-assists). That’s why “impact” models incorporate plus-minus and tracking. PER persists because it’s simple to quote, available historically, and correlates with “star-ness” in many eras. It’s not the best tool, but it’s a familiar baseline.
Suppose I’m analyzing a draft class historically. Is PER useful there since it’s available back decades?

It can be useful as a rough filter, with caveats. Pros for historical/draft-class retrospectives: - Available across long time spans - Per-minute normalization helps compare players with different minutes - Captures some broad “did this guy stuff the box score?” signal Caveats: - Era and role differences: the mapping from box-score stats to value changes (e.g., three-point revolution, pace, defensive rules). - Team context: some players’ roles inflate certain stats. - Defensive evaluation is weak. - Survivorship bias: players who stick around accumulate the minutes and roles that make PER look good. If you’re doing historical comparisons, I’d recommend using a basket: - rTS for efficiency relative to era - BPM (available back to 1973-74 in B-Ref’s version) for impact-ish - WS/48 for an efficiency/wins lens - Plus-minus where available (more limited historically) And then interpret together rather than crown one number.
Can you explain the relationship between WS/48 and BPM? Sometimes they disagree wildly.

They disagree because they’re measuring different things and using different inputs. WS/48: - A rate of “wins allocated per 48 minutes” - Anchored to team efficiency and win estimation - Defense heavily team-context-driven - Rewards playing on teams with strong net efficiency, and rewards efficient scoring and low turnovers on offense BPM: - Estimated plus-minus per 100 possessions from box score - Trained to match adjusted plus-minus patterns historically - More sensitive to certain box features that correlate with impact (assist rate, rebounding, etc.) - Not directly anchored to team wins, but calibrated to team net rating totals So disagreements happen when: - A player is on a great team with a role that WS likes (efficient finishing, low mistakes) but their box profile doesn’t scream “driver of impact,” so BPM is modest. - A player has a box profile that BPM thinks correlates with impact (high assists, rebounds, steals) but is on a mediocre team or has efficiency issues that reduce WS. - Defensive context: WS can credit defense to minutes on an elite defense; BPM might not give as much if the box doesn’t show it. Also, WS/48 can be “compressed” for certain roles; BPM can be more extreme for certain stat profiles.
What about VORP? Is it just BPM times minutes?

Pretty close. VORP (Value Over Replacement Player) on Basketball-Reference is derived from BPM and playing time, with a replacement-level baseline. Conceptually: VORP ≈ (BPM − replacement_level) * minutes * scaling Replacement level is set below average (negative BPM). The idea is to estimate cumulative value above a readily available bench/waiver player. So: - BPM is rate impact (per 100 possessions) - VORP is cumulative impact above replacement It’s useful for season totals and career totals, but it inherits BPM’s box-score limitations and any calibration quirks.
Can you talk about “replacement level” and why it matters? People throw it around but it seems arbitrary.

Replacement level is a conceptual baseline: the level of performance you could get from readily available players with minimal acquisition cost (end-of-bench, two-way types, etc.). It’s below league average because teams have limited roster spots and minutes; not everyone who plays is replacement-level. Why it matters: - It separates “above average” from “valuable relative to what you could easily substitute.” - It allows cumulative metrics to reflect scarcity: playing 2,000 minutes at slightly above replacement is valuable, even if not “above average.” Why it’s arbitrary: - The exact cutoff is a modeling choice. Is replacement -2 points per 100? -2.5? It depends on league structure, talent distribution, and how you want the metric to behave. - Different metrics choose different baselines. WAR-style metrics in other sports have similar debates. The important part is consistency: within a metric, replacement level is chosen so that league totals and team totals make sense and the distribution matches intuitive roster value.
Let’s do a concrete example. Construct two hypothetical players and show why PER might pick one and BPM might pick the other.

Sure—two per-36 stat lines, same minutes, simplified. Player A (Box-stuffer big archetype): - 24 points on 20 FGA, 6 FTA - 10 rebounds (8 D, 2 O) - 2 assists - 1 steal, 2 blocks - 3 turnovers - 3 fouls Player B (Connector wing archetype): - 16 points on 10 FGA, 4 FTA (high efficiency) - 5 rebounds - 6 assists - 2 steals, 0 blocks - 1 turnover - 2 fouls PER tendencies: - Player A: lots of points + rebounds + blocks; the missed shots and turnovers hurt but may not outweigh the volume of “credited” events. PER will likely be high. - Player B: fewer points and rebounds, though efficient; assists and steals help, low turnovers help. PER might still be good, but depending on weights, it may not match A’s “big counting stats” profile. BPM tendencies: - Player B’s assists, steals, low turnovers, and efficiency can map strongly to impact in the regression, especially if the model thinks those correlate with driving offense and preventing points. - Player A’s rebounds/blocks help, but if the scoring is inefficient and turnovers are meaningful, BPM may be less impressed. So PER might rank A above B due to raw box accumulation; BPM might rank B above A due to box features that historically correlate with net rating impact. This is stylized, but it captures the typical disagreement.
How do turnovers get treated across these metrics? I feel like some models “forgive” turnovers for creators, others don’t.

Turnovers are tricky because: - They directly end possessions (big negative) - But high creation roles inherently involve more risk - Some turnovers are “productive failures” (attempted high-value passes) compared to sloppy mistakes, and box score doesn’t differentiate PER: penalizes turnovers fairly strongly, and it doesn’t explicitly credit “creation difficulty” beyond assists and made shots. So high-turnover creators can look worse than their impact suggests. Win Shares: turnovers reduce offensive efficiency and possessions; it penalizes them via offensive rating/points produced framework. But if the creator generates lots of points and assists efficiently, WS can still be strong. BPM: because it’s trained on impact, it can implicitly “forgive” some turnovers if, historically, players with that turnover profile also had high plus-minus impact due to creation. But it still penalizes turnovers; it’s just that the net coefficient environment can be more nuanced. Modern impact metrics: often use priors and lineup data, so if the creator’s teams score well with them despite turnovers, the metric will rate them highly.
What about assists? Box score assists seem “noisy” because of scorer bias and system effects. How do metrics account for that?

Assists have several issues: - Scorer interpretation variance (historically more than now) - System effects: some offenses generate more assisted shots - “Assist opportunity” depends on teammates’ shooting - Assists ignore advantage creation that doesn’t end in the immediate pass-to-bucket PER: includes an assist component with a team assist factor adjustment, trying to dampen system effects. It’s still crude. WS: uses “points produced” which credits assists as producing points, but again it’s dependent on teammates converting. BPM: uses assists as a strong predictor of offensive impact, with position adjustments. It doesn’t directly correct scorer bias, but across big datasets, that noise is smaller than the signal. Better approaches: - Use play-by-play derived “potential assists,” “secondary assists,” “passes leading to free throws,” etc. Those require tracking. - Use on/off and lineup data to see whether team offense improves. In public box-score-only land, the best you can do is combine assists with turnover rate, usage, and efficiency to infer decision quality.
You keep mentioning “spacing” as invisible to box score. Is there any proxy for it in public stats?

Proxies exist but they’re imperfect. 1) Three-point attempt rate and accuracy A high 3PA rate at good percentage suggests a player provides spacing. But “gravity” depends on defender behavior, not just makes. 2) Team on/off shooting and rim attempts If lineups with the player see more rim attempts or better efficiency, that can hint at spacing. But that’s confounded by teammates. 3) Free throw rate for teammates Sometimes spacing correlates with teammates getting cleaner drives, which can show up in teammate rim frequency or free throws. Again confounded. 4) Tracking-based proxies (if you have them) Things like “defender distance,” “double-team rate,” “off-ball screen frequency,” etc. But you asked for public and non-proprietary; some tracking summaries are public but not always easily downloadable historically. So in box-score-only evaluation, spacing is largely qualitative unless you incorporate lineup data.
Okay, let’s do lineup data. How should a stat-head use on/off without getting fooled by noise?

On/off is seductive and dangerous. Best practices: 1) Use multi-year samples where possible Single-season on/off can swing due to shooting variance. 2) Contextualize with who shares minutes Raw on/off confounds teammates and opponents. If a player plays mostly with starters and sits with bench lineups, their on/off will look great. 3) Use “with or without you” splits Look at how lineups perform with the player and each key teammate separately. This begins to isolate but still noisy. 4) Use adjusted plus-minus (APM/RAPM) if available That’s the formal way to adjust for lineup context. 5) Focus on components, not just net Is the on/off driven by offense or defense? Is it driven by opponent 3P% (high variance) or by turnover rate (more stable)? 6) Regression to the mean Treat extreme on/off as a clue to investigate, not a conclusion. 7) Minutes thresholds Very low-minute lineup data is mostly noise. In short: on/off is a diagnostic, not a final metric.
What’s more stable year-to-year: shooting, rebounding, passing, or defense? I’m thinking about what to “trust” in projections.

Generally, in terms of statistical stability (not “importance”), rough ordering for individuals: More stable: - Free throw percentage (surprisingly stable) - Three-point percentage is moderately stable but needs volume; the signal-to-noise improves with attempts - Role-based rates like assist rate can be fairly stable for established roles - Rebounding rates (especially defensive rebounding) are relatively stable given role/position Less stable: - Steal and block rates are somewhat stable but can vary with scheme/role - On/off defensive impact is noisy - Opponent shooting percentages when a player is on the court are very noisy But context matters: if a player changes role, team, or minutes, stability changes. For projections, many models use Bayesian shrinkage (pulling observed rates toward a prior based on position/age) and weight recent seasons more.
Since you brought up Bayesian shrinkage, can you explain how that would apply to something like 3P%?

Suppose a player shoots 40% from three on 100 attempts. Is that “true talent” 40%? Probably not exactly; there’s variance. A Bayesian approach: - Prior: league-average 3P% for similar players (say 35%), with a prior strength equivalent to, say, 200 attempts. - Likelihood: observed makes out of attempts (binomial). - Posterior mean: weighted average of prior mean and observed rate, weighted by attempt counts. Posterior mean ≈ (prior_attempts * prior_pct + observed_attempts * observed_pct) / (prior_attempts + observed_attempts) Plug in: (200*0.35 + 100*0.40) / 300 = (70 + 40) / 300 = 0.3667 So you’d project ~36.7% rather than 40% unless there’s more evidence (more attempts, multiple seasons, or additional info like shot quality). This logic underpins why small-sample spikes shouldn’t be taken at face value.
How does that relate to PER or WS? Do those stats do shrinkage?

Traditional PER and WS as published are not Bayesian; they’re deterministic formulas applied to observed box scores. They don’t shrink noisy components toward priors. Some modern “forecast” systems do apply shrinkage to the inputs (shooting rates, rebound rates, etc.) and then compute derived values, or they directly model outcomes (like future BPM) with shrinkage and aging curves. So if you’re using PER/WS for prediction, you’d want to: - Use multi-year averages (minutes-weighted) - Regress extreme rates (3P%, steal rate, etc.) toward league norms based on sample size - Adjust for age and role changes But the published PER/WS numbers themselves aren’t shrinkage-adjusted.
Let’s talk about pace. You said PER adjusts for pace. How exactly does pace confound per-minute stats?

Pace is possessions per game. If a team plays faster, there are more possessions per minute of game time. More possessions mean more opportunities for: - shots - rebounds - assists - turnovers - steals/blocks Per-minute stats will inflate on faster teams even if per-possession contribution is the same. Example: Two players each contribute 0.20 “value units” per possession. Player on a 102 pace team will accumulate more per minute than player on a 96 pace team, simply due to more possessions. So for fair comparison, you want per-possession (per 100 possessions) or pace-adjusted per-minute. PER tries to correct per-minute inflation with a pace factor. BPM is already per 100 possessions, so it’s inherently pace-neutral, though calibration still matters.
If BPM is per 100 possessions, why do people still quote per-game stats so much? Isn’t that just narrative?

Per-game stats persist because: - They’re intuitive and easy to visualize (25 points per game “feels” like a lot) - Awards/media historically emphasize per-game - Per-game aligns with typical consumption: watching games, you see totals But for analysis: - Per-possession or per-minute is better for comparing across pace and minutes differences. - Per-game can be useful for workload and durability narratives (how much a player provides in a typical game), but it mixes rate and minutes. A good compromise is: - Rate: per 75 possessions or per 36 minutes for skill - Volume: minutes and games for availability - Efficiency: TS% and turnover rate - Impact: plus-minus metrics
What about “points per 75”? Why 75?

It’s a convenience normalization roughly approximating a star’s possessions in a game. It’s similar to per-36, but possessions-based rather than minutes-based, so it adjusts for pace and for how many possessions a player is on the floor. Per-75 possessions is popular because: - It’s close to “per game” scale for many starters without being dependent on actual minutes - It makes comparisons across teams/eras cleaner than per-game - It aligns with impact metrics that are per 100 possessions You could use per-100 as well; per-75 is just a scaling choice.
Let’s go deeper on “possession used.” Usage rate isn’t actually possessions, right?

Correct—usage rate (USG%) is an estimate of the share of team possessions a player “uses” while on the floor, where “use” is defined as ending a possession with: - a field goal attempt - free throw attempts (with a factor) - a turnover A common formula: USG% = 100 * ((FGA + 0.44*FTA + TOV) * (team minutes)) / (minutes played * (team FGA + 0.44*team FTA + team TOV)) So it’s not literal possessions touched; it’s possessions finished. It misses: - possessions where the player passes and someone else shoots - screen/spacing contributions - possessions where the player initiates but doesn’t take the final shot or commit the turnover It’s still useful as a role indicator: high USG means primary finisher/creator; low USG means more of a complementary role.
How does that interact with assist rate? Like two players can have same USG but different assist profiles.

Exactly, and that’s why analysts use “usage + assist” frameworks. Two players with 28% USG: - Player X: high assist rate, high creation; they end possessions often but also generate shots for others. - Player Y: lower assist rate; more of a scorer who ends possessions with their own shots/free throws. To capture this, you can use: - Assist percentage (AST%) - “Usage + AST%” as a proxy for offensive load - Or more refined: “Box Creation” models (estimating shots created via passing and scoring gravity), though those can be semi-proprietary. In impact terms, high-usage low-assist players need great efficiency to be top-tier offensively; high-usage high-assist players can drive elite offenses even with slightly lower scoring efficiency if they elevate teammates.
Do PER or WS incorporate assist rate in a way that captures that distinction?

Partially. PER: - Gives explicit credit for assists with some team adjustment. So high-assist players benefit. - But it can still prefer scorers because points and rebounds are heavily weighted, depending on position. WS: - Credits assists via points produced, but it also depends on team shooting converting those assisted shots. - It tends to reward efficient scoring and low turnovers, and it doesn’t fully capture “advantage creation” beyond direct assists. Neither fully captures the nuance of “offense runs through you” beyond assists and usage. BPM does a bit better because it’s trained on impact and includes assists in a role-adjusted way, but it still can’t see pre-assists, spacing, or the degree of defensive attention directly.
If you had to design a simple open metric today, using only box score and play-by-play, what would you do?

A reasonable “simple but modern” open metric using box + play-by-play (no tracking) might be: 1) Build offensive value from: - Scoring efficiency: points per shot attempt (TS-based) relative to league average, scaled by usage - Playmaking: assists with a turnover adjustment, possibly using estimated points created from assists (2.3-ish points per assist adjusted by team shot mix) - Offensive rebounds: value as extra possessions, with league average points per possession multiplier - Turnovers: negative value equal to average points per possession 2) Build defensive value from: - Play-by-play on/off defensive rating adjusted for teammates/opponents (a RAPM-lite), with heavy regularization - Add box priors: steals/blocks/defensive rebounds as weak priors to stabilize small samples 3) Combine with a Bayesian prior and aging curve for stability - Shrink toward position/role priors - Use multi-year blending 4) Output in points per 100 possessions above average, plus a wins conversion: wins ≈ points_added / (points_per_win) That would essentially recreate the philosophy of EPM/LEBRON in a simplified open way: use plus-minus as the target, box as features/prior, and strong regularization.
That’s basically “do an impact model.” But what if I insist on no regression, just a formula?

Then you’re back in linear weights territory. A formula approach: - Assign each box event a points value: - Made 3: +3 minus expected points of a shot attempt (so net above baseline) - Made 2: +2 minus baseline - Misses: negative baseline - FT made/missed similarly - Offensive rebound: + (league points per possession * continuation factor) - Defensive rebound: ends opponent possession: + (league points per possession prevented), though you must avoid double-counting with missed shots - Turnover: − (league points per possession) - Steal: turnover forced: + (league PPP) plus maybe transition bonus - Block: partial credit; not all blocks change possession - Fouls: depends; could be negative based on free throws yielded (not in box score cleanly) But the challenge is double-counting and attribution: - Rebounds depend on misses - Assists depend on makes - Blocks don’t always change possession - Fouls’ value depends on bonus situation and FT outcomes You can still build a coherent formula, but it becomes complicated quickly. Regression models handle these dependencies implicitly by learning weights from outcomes.
All right. Let’s do a critique exercise. Give me a list of “common mistakes” fans make when using PER/WS/BPM.

Common mistakes: 1) Treating PER as “overall value” It’s productivity per minute from box score, not impact. 2) Comparing WS totals without considering minutes and team strength WS is cumulative and team-anchored; totals favor players with more minutes on better teams. 3) Using WS/48 as a pure “talent” metric It can be influenced by team context and role; it’s not purely individual. 4) Treating DBPM or DWS as definitive defense grades They’re noisy and proxy-based. 5) Ignoring era and position A PER of 20 in one era may represent different shot profiles and league efficiencies than another era; similarly, comparing guards and centers without role context can mislead. 6) Overreacting to single-season BPM swings BPM can move with role changes and shooting variance. 7) Not separating rate vs volume A high BPM in limited minutes doesn’t automatically mean the player could sustain it in a larger role. 8) Cherry-picking one metric Metrics disagree; you should triangulate. 9) Ignoring uncertainty All these numbers have error bars, especially in small samples.
Speaking of error bars, are there published error estimates for these metrics?

For PER/WS/BPM as published, no formal error bars are typically provided because they’re deterministic transformations of observed stats. For plus-minus metrics like RAPM, you can compute standard errors from the regression (and in ridge regression there are ways to approximate uncertainty), and some public RAPM tables include confidence intervals. For hybrid metrics like EPM or DARKO, uncertainty might be partially modeled internally, but public outputs often don’t provide full error bars. As an analyst, you can approximate uncertainty by: - Looking at year-to-year stability - Using bootstrap resampling on lineup data (for plus-minus) - Treating small samples as high variance and shrinking toward priors
I want to understand one particular weirdness: PER seems to love defensive rebounds. But defensive rebounds are partly “team scheme” and shot profile. Why weight them so much?

Defensive rebounds are valued because ending the opponent’s possession is valuable, and the box score records the rebounder as the one who secured it. PER gives credit because it’s measuring box-score “productivity,” and rebounds are a big, consistent stat category. But you’re right: defensive rebounds are context-dependent: - Team schemes can funnel rebounds to certain positions (boxing out by one player, rebound by another). - Opponent shot profile affects rebound opportunities (more threes → longer rebounds, different distribution). - Some defensive possessions end in turnovers or made shots, reducing rebound chances. So heavy weighting can over-credit players who collect easy defensive boards in favorable contexts. Modern impact models often find that defensive rebounding is valuable but not as individually differentiating as raw totals suggest—especially once you adjust for role. Offensive rebounds are generally more individually valuable and less scheme-allocated, though still context-dependent.
How do you think about “positional adjustment” in metrics? Like, should a guard’s rebound be worth more than a center’s?

There are two different questions: 1) Intrinsic value of the event A rebound ends/extends a possession regardless of who gets it. Intrinsically, an additional defensive rebound is worth roughly the average points prevented from a continued opponent possession (which is basically ending the possession), and an offensive rebound is worth roughly the average points you’ll score on the extended possession. 2) Marginal/rarity and opportunity cost by position A guard rebound might be rarer and could indicate skill or scheme (crashing), but it might also come at an opportunity cost (getting back on defense, though that’s outside box score). A center rebound is more expected given positioning. Position adjustment in metrics like BPM isn’t saying the rebound is “worth more points” for a guard; it’s saying that, historically, a guard with high rebound rate correlates with certain impact patterns that the model tries to capture. For a pure linear weights “points added” model, you’d value rebounds similarly across positions, but you might adjust for context and double-counting. For an impact prediction model, you might weight differently because of correlation with unobserved skills.
I’ve heard that PER has an “assist adjustment” that penalizes players on high-assist teams. Is that true?

PER uses a team assist factor to adjust the value of assists (and possibly related scoring credit) to account for team context. The idea is: if a team’s offense generates a lot of assists, an individual assist might be “less exceptional” and more system-driven, so it slightly reduces the marginal credit of each assist; conversely, on a low-assist team, assists might be more individually meaningful. In practice, this can produce unintuitive effects: - Two players with identical box lines on different teams can have slightly different uPER due to team factors. - It can feel like it’s penalizing players for being in a good system. Whether you think that’s “right” depends on your philosophy. Many modern analysts would rather model impact directly than adjust assists with team factors in a heuristic way.
That seems like a strange assumption. Wouldn’t a high-assist team also have more assist opportunities, making assists easier to get?

Exactly—that’s the tension. High-assist environments can inflate assist counts because: - More assisted shot attempts - More motion leading to higher-quality shots - Better finishers converting passes So you could argue for devaluing assists in such contexts if you’re trying to isolate individual creation. But you could also argue the opposite: being a high-assist team might reflect collective passing skill, and an individual’s assists still represent real value. The more defensible approach is to: - Use play-by-play and tracking to measure “potential assists,” “assist points created,” and passing difficulty, or - Use impact models that “see” whether the offense improves with the player on the court, regardless of team assist rate. PER’s team adjustment is a blunt tool that tries to correct something real but can misattribute.
Let’s switch gears to shot value. How do you evaluate a scorer beyond TS%? Like, what about shot difficulty and playoff translation?

Beyond TS%, you’d look at: 1) Shot profile - 3PA rate and accuracy - Rim attempts and finishing - Midrange frequency and accuracy (often less efficient, but can matter if it’s self-created and resilient) 2) Self-creation vs assisted - Percent of makes assisted (requires play-by-play/tracking summaries) - Higher self-creation can be more valuable because it scales in tougher contexts. 3) Free throw rate - FTr = FTA/FGA or FTA per 100 possessions - Getting to the line is a stable way to generate efficient points and can be valuable when defenses tighten. 4) Turnover rate and passing out of pressure - A scorer who can pass when help comes adds value even if TS is similar. 5) Scalability - Can they maintain efficiency at higher volume? - Can they fit with other stars (off-ball value, catch-and-shoot ability)? 6) Resilience indicators (imperfect) - Performance vs top defenses - Efficiency on high-leverage possessions (clutch is noisy, but you can look at usage/turnovers under pressure) - Shot diet diversity: reliance on one shot type can be easier to scheme against. Without tracking, you’re limited, but you can still combine shot mix stats (rim/3/mid splits) with assisted rates if available.
Since we’re avoiding proprietary stuff, what public data sources can I use to compute these things?

Public-ish sources: - Basketball-Reference: - Basic and advanced stats (PER, WS, BPM, TS%, rTS, usage, etc.) - Play-by-play tables (on/off, lineup summaries in some form) - Shot distance splits (2P by distance buckets in some eras) - NBA’s public stats site: - Has shot dashboards, passing, touches, shooting by distance, etc. It’s “public” but sometimes rate-limited and not always stable for scraping. - pbpstats (site) and its data tools: - Aggregated play-by-play derived stats and lineup data. - StatsBomb-style open data doesn’t exist for NBA at full granularity, but there are community projects and GitHub repos with scraped play-by-play. For a stat-head project, you can start with B-Ref exports and pbpstats for lineup/off-on and then compute your own derived metrics.
If you had to explain PER and WS to a non-analytics fan in one sentence each, what would you say?

PER: “A per-minute index of box-score production, normalized so league average is 15.” Win Shares: “An estimate of how many team wins a player ‘accounted for,’ allocated from team offense and defense using box-score stats.”
Last question for now: if I’m building a model to predict team wins, should I use PER or WS as features?

If the target is team wins, WS has an obvious alignment because it’s literally derived from team efficiency and wins allocation—so it can be circular if you use it to predict the same season’s wins. For prediction (future wins), WS from previous seasons could be useful as a summary of past performance, but it carries team context. PER can be a feature as a summary of box productivity, but it’s not directly tied to winning. For a predictive model, better features would be: - Player-level: minutes projections, age, TS%, usage, turnover rate, 3P volume/accuracy, rebounding rates, prior impact metrics (BPM, RAPM variants if available) - Team-level: continuity, coaching/system proxies (hard), schedule strength If you want a single “past value” feature, multi-year BPM or a stabilized impact metric tends to outperform PER/WS in predicting future point differential, which then predicts wins.
I’m going to push back: isn’t BPM also partially circular since it’s calibrated to team net rating? If I use it to predict team net rating, aren’t I cheating?

Good pushback. There are two flavors of “circularity”: 1) Identity-level circularity (hard circular) WS is computed from team efficiency and wins, then allocated to players. Summing WS is designed to reproduce team wins. Using same-season WS to “predict” same-season wins is basically an identity. 2) Calibration-level alignment (soft circular) BPM is calibrated so that minute-weighted team BPM totals align with team net rating in aggregate. But BPM is still computed from player box stats via a model, not from team net rating directly. So it’s less of a hard identity, but it does mean that team totals are constrained. If you use same-season BPM to predict same-season net rating, you’re using information that is highly correlated and partly constrained to match. For prediction, you should use prior-season BPM (or a projection) and projected minutes. That avoids cheating. In general: - Predicting contemporaneous team performance from player metrics computed from that same season risks leakage. - Use lagged stats and projections.
Makes sense. Next time I want to talk about building a simple projection system using only public data. But for now, can you summarize the key takeaways about PER vs WS vs BPM?

PER: box-score productivity per minute with heuristic weights; good for “who stuffs the box,” biased by role/position and weak on defense/spacing. WS: allocates team wins into player shares; good for historical framing and team-success alignment, but defense is heavily team/minutes driven and it’s context-heavy. BPM: box-score-based estimate of plus-minus impact per 100 possessions; more “impact-oriented” than PER/WS, still limited by box score, but generally a better single-number baseline. If you want one number for modern analysis, prefer an impact metric; if you want to understand why, break it into efficiency, usage, playmaking, turnovers, and rebounding, then check lineup context.
